Switching Costs

Killer prices drive customers to try Lemonade, but it's the technology that keeps them loyal for the long haul.

Once customers try the Lemonade experience, I think they are more inclined to stay as customers than to switch to another provider.

Think about it: with rock-bottom prices in place, a wonderfully designed mobile app, and a seamlessly instantaneous claims process, why would customers leave Lemonade and sign up with a traditional insurer, which is typically more expensive, less user-friendly, and much slower?

Perhaps, they would leave because Lemonade's insurance does not have enough coverage. This could be true since Lemonade focuses on offering basic coverage, thus its lower price points. But as Lemonade scales and grows its customer base and collects more data, its technology and AI will become even smarter and more efficient, thus amplifying its cost advantage moat. This allows Lemonade to expand its coverage offerings while maintaining its already-low prices, which ultimately makes Lemonade insurance a superior product compared to incumbents. So this problem of having insurance policies that are "too basic" should eventually be alleviated as Lemonade matures.

Another reason why customers might leave Lemonade is due to the absence of live agents. This is a double-edged sword for Lemonade — while Lemonade relies on bots to automatically onboard new clients, process claims, and answer customer inquiries, the lack of human interaction may be frustrating for some. Yet again, I'm sure the majority of customers prefer to get their claims paid out instantly, instead of waiting for days, weeks, and months to get their claims approved and sent to them, which, by the way, usually involves numerous back-and-forth with an agent.

I understand that having a wide enough coverage and access to human agents are important factors for some policyholders but for the overwhelming majority of people who just want basic coverage at killer prices, I think staying on the Lemonade platform is a no-brainer.

In addition, as customers remain longer with Lemonade, they are more likely to adopt more products under the Lemonade umbrella. Multi-product adoption benefits Lemonade in two ways:

  1. Increases premium per customer. For instance, in 2024, 17% of Lemonade's pet insurance policies were cross-sold to existing customers, leading to a 3.7x increase in median premium per customer despite minimal incremental customer acquisition costs.
  2. Increases switching costs for customers. Bundling multiple insurance offerings typically saves them more money, as opposed to having different insurance products across multiple insurance carriers. This makes them even more stubborn to leave Lemonade, thus enhancing platform stickiness.

We can see that Lemonade's switching cost moat is getting stronger over time. Annual Dollar Retention, defined as the percentage of In-Force Premium retained over the last twelve months, was 86% as of Q4 of 2024, an increase from 81% in Q1 of 2021. This is despite Lemonade deliberately not renewing certain policies that failed to meet its underwriting criteria over the last few quarters.

I expect this metric to reach 90%+ in the long term. That said, the company's overall ADR trend is up, signifying a strengthening of its switching cost moat.

All in all, Lemonade's brand, cost advantage, and switching cost moats should help the company gain market share, ultimately driving sustainable, profitable growth for many years to come.

Growth: Squeezing Out the Competition

Before we dive into Lemonade's financials, let me briefly talk about Lemonade's business model.

At the core of Lemonade’s business model is reinsurance. Basically, reinsurance is "insurance for insurance companies". Under a reinsurance contract, Lemonade transfers, or "cedes", a percentage of the premium to reinsurers. In return, these reinsurers agree to pay Lemonade a "ceding commission" as well as cover a portion of the corresponding claims.

Here's a simplified chart of how reinsurance works:

Initially, Lemonade cedes 75% of the premium to reinsurers, while retaining 25% of the premium for operating expenses and potential profits. The higher the percentage it cedes to reinsurers, the more "protected" the business is from potential losses, but this consequently lowers its Revenue and earnings potential.

Over the years, Lemonade's underwriting and risk management models have gotten smarter and better, which allowed Lemonade to reduce its quota share protection to just 55% as of July 2024. This also means that Lemonade gets to keep the remaining 45% of the premium, thus increasing its earnings potential.

Fundamentally, buying reinsurance serves two purposes for Lemonade:

  • Maximize Capital Efficiency: in most jurisdictions, insurers are required to keep significant capital reserves to withstand periods of elevated levels of claims due to, for instance, natural disasters. By sharing premiums, Lemonade shifts most of the capital reserve requirement to the reinsurer, thus allowing Lemonade to stay relatively capital-light.
  • Optimize Gross Margin: ceding a portion of its premiums to reinsurers reduces bottom-line volatility as reinsurers, not Lemonade, have to cover most of the claims. At the same time, Lemonade has also signed reinsurance agreements to reduce the maximum amount that Lemonade has to pay for any one claim, thus shielding Lemonade from extreme losses.

In short, Lemonade's reinsurance model allows it "to architect our business to be at once capital-light and possessed of a predictable and growing gross margin".

Also worth mentioning is that Lemonade operates a subscription-based model, which creates a highly recurring, high-quality income stream for the business.

Having said that, here are the different components that make up Lemonade's Revenue:

  • Net Earned Premium: This represents the earned portion of Lemonade's Gross Written Premium minus the earned portion of the premium ceded to reinsurers.
  • Ceding Commission Income: This is the commission Lemonade receives from reinsurers for ceding a portion of its Gross Written Premium. This income compensates Lemonade for the costs of acquiring customers and underwriting policies.
  • Net Investment Income: This is interest income generated from investing in securities like bonds, Treasury bills, or money market funds. Insurance companies typically use "float" — premiums collected from policyholders that are sitting idle on the balance sheet before they are used to pay out claims or other obligations — to invest in securities to generate investment income.
  • Commission and Other Income: This consists of commission earned from placing policies with third-party insurers and fees collected from policyholders relating to installment premiums.

With these in mind, let's dive into Lemonade's financials, starting with growth.

Looking at the company's topline, In Force Premium — or the total annualized premium for all active policies — was $944M as of Q4, up 26% YoY. This was slightly ahead of management's guidance of $938M to $942M set at the beginning of 2024, reflecting the strength and predictability of Lemonade's business model.

While it is nowhere near pre-2023 growth rates, it is still encouraging to see IFP growth accelerating in the last few quarters, pointing to a recovery in business momentum and increased demand for Lemonade's insurance products.

Breaking it down by product type, Lemonade's $944M of IFP consists of:

  • Homeowners insurance of $503M, up 16% YoY
  • Pet insurance of $283M, up 57% YoY.
  • Car insurance of $122M, up 7% YoY.
  • European insurance of $24M, up 140% YoY
  • Other insurance of $12M, up 33% YoY.

Of the three core products, we can see that Pet is growing the fastest, followed by the legacy Homeowners segment. Car, on the other hand, has been slow to gain traction. However, we should see the Car segment step on the gas pedal (pun intended) as Lemonade continues to expand operations nationwide. Just recently, Lemonade Car expanded to Colorado, where people spend $7B on car insurance annually. This also brings Lemonade's exposure to approximately 40% of the US car insurance market — still a lot of room to grow.

Moving forward, we can expect IFP growth to continue to accelerate in Q1. For one, management recently announced that Lemonade has crossed $1B of IFP, which has surpassed their Q1 guidance of $997M to $1B. This equates to a 26% YoY growth over Q1 of 2024, but since this was announced on March 25th (one week before Q1 closes), we will likely see Q1 IFP to the tune of $1.02B, which is about a 28% YoY growth.

For the full year, management expects an IFP of $1.203B to $1.208B, representing a 28% YoY growth. Beyond that, management expects "sustained acceleration towards our cruising velocity above 30%, expected in 2026". In other words, we can expect robust IFP growth ahead for at least the next two or three years.

That said, IFP growth was largely driven by higher customer count. In Q4, Total Customers were about 2.4M, up 20% YoY. Improved monetization also contributed to IFP growth. In Q4, Premium Per Customer was up 5% YoY to $388 due to increased multi-product adoption, growth in average policy value, and a continued mix shift toward higher value policies.

The number of Customers on a platform is probably the most important metric to track as it is a leading indicator of future IFP growth, and subsequently, Revenue growth. Thus, seeing Lemonade continue to grow its customer base with each passing quarter is a testament to its strong value proposition and increasing Revenue potential.

On that note, Q4 Revenue was $149M, up 29% YoY with growth accelerating 10pp QoQ. This was driven by higher IFP, which consequently led to robust growth across all of Lemonade's Revenue segments.

  • Net Earned Premium of $101M, up 21% YoY.
  • Ceding Commission Income of $30M, up 61% YoY.
  • Net Investment Income of $9M, up 32% YoY.
  • Commission and Other Income of $9M, up 41% YoY.

In terms of the Revenue outlook, management expects Q1 Revenue of $143M to $145M, implying a 22% YoY growth. If management meets this guidance, it means that Revenue is set to decline on a QoQ basis from $149M in Q4. Correct me if I'm wrong, but this will also mark the first quarter ever since the company went public, where Revenue declined on a sequential basis.

This was a head-scratcher for me because management guided for a sequential increase in IFP and Gross Earned Premium, which should theoretically lead to an expansion in Revenue. However, management is expecting Revenue to decline, which also invalidates Lemonade's "highly recurring and naturally growing Revenue streams".

I think two factors might have led to this phenomenon: 1) the intentional removal of higher-risk homeowners policies from its book, and 2) the unfavorable timing of Ceding Commission Income paid to Lemonade.

That said, analysts expect Q1 Revenue of $145M, which is the high end of management's guidance, but do consider the fact that Lemonade has a 100% track record of beating analyst Revenue estimates ever since it went public. Given management's strong execution and Lemonade's recent IFP milestone of $1B, I believe Q1 Revenue will come in ahead of management's guidance — I'd say at least $150M.

For the full year, management expects 2025 Revenue of $655M to $657M, implying a 25% YoY growth at the midpoint.

Whatever it is, Lemonade has a massive growth runaway ahead as it continues to disrupt the legacy insurance industry. Here's how Lemonade can grow further:

  1. Acquiring first-time buyers: According to Lemonade, 92% of its home and renters customers did not switch to Lemonade from another carrier — meaning, they are first-time insurance buyers. Lemonade clearly resonates with the price-sensitive, underserved population, which should help the company capture this demographic for years to come.
  2. Cross-selling: As a household grows, customers will inevitably need additional coverage, whether it be for a new home, car, or pet. By tapping its existing user base, Lemonade can cross-sell its product at minimal cost. This applies to new insurance products as well. For instance, of the ~2.4M Lemonade customers, about 1.2M of them already have car insurance with another carrier. Assuming an average annual premium of $2,000 per customer (Lemonade's Car Premium Per Customer is currently $1,800), it would translate to $2.4B of incremental car premiums at no additional acquisition costs.
  3. Expand Geographically: As I've mentioned earlier, Lemonade Car is available in only 40% of the US car insurance market, so Lemonade can gradually expand to other states to increase its growth exposure. Furthermore, Lemonade is still a tiny business in Europe, with only $24M of IFP as of Q4.
  4. Partnerships: Expanding its partner network could also boost Lemonade's brand awareness and Revenue growth. For instance, partnering with ride-hailing companies like Uber and Lyft could supercharge Lemonade's growth.

Without a doubt, car insurance will serve as the reactive agent that will propel the Lemonade business to new levels — one that will 10x the business in the next decade or so, to $10B of IFP.

But consider this as well. Lemonade has 2.4M Customers. The US has 340M people. Let's say there are 250M adults in the US. That means Lemonade has a market penetration rate of less than 1%.

Given Lemonade's strong brand and ability to scale some of its earlier products, namely pet and renters to 5% to 8% market share, I believe Lemonade has what it takes to squeeze out the competition and 10x its business over the long run — and maybe more.

It will surely be a long and difficult road, but it will be a prize worth fighting for.

The world spends more money on insurance than on advertising, aerospace, agriculture, artificial intelligence, automotive, cloud-computing, cybersecurity, defense, education, electronics, entertainment, fashion & apparel, food & beverages, oil & gas, renewable energy, pharma, semiconductors, shipping, software, sports, telecom, tourism, transportation,

In short: Insurance is a prize worth fighting for.

(Lemonade Investor Day 2024)

Profitability: Getting Sweeter

Lemonade's cost structure consists of five main components. The first two make up the company's Cost of Revenue, while the last three make up its Operating Expenses.

  • Loss and Loss Adjustment Expense, Net
  • Other Insurance Expense
  • Sales & Marketing
  • Research & Development
  • General & Administrative

Growth makes little sense if Lemonade does not have an efficient cost structure, and thus, is unable to turn a profit. Although Lemonade is still unprofitable at the moment, the company is well on track to achieving durable profitable growth.

For one, Lemonade is gaining significant economies of scale, as evidenced by its improved unit economics.

In Q4, Gross Profit was $64M, up 90% YoY, with Gross Margin expanding 14pp YoY and 16pp QoQ, to an all-time high of 43%. This was mainly driven by higher IFP and improved loss ratios.

Likewise, Adjusted Gross Profit — Gross Profit excluding Net Investment Income, Interest Income, and Other Income, but adds back some non-direct expenses — was $66M, up 88% YoY. In essence, Adjusted Gross Profit is the money left after growing its core insurance business and paying variable costs.

Adjusted Gross Profit as a % of Gross Earned Premium was 29% in Q4, improving 9pp YoY and 11pp QoQ. Gross Earned Premium is used as the denominator (instead of Gross Written Premium) as it excludes the impact of premiums ceded to reinsurers, thus reflecting Lemonade-only volume and the unit economics from that volume. As you can see below, Lemonade's Adjusted Gross Profit as a % of Gross Earned Premium is at an all-time high, indicating higher earnings potential.

Another way of examining Lemonade's unit economics is to look at its loss ratios. The lower they are, the more profitable Lemonade is — impressively, Lemonade's loss ratios are at an all-time low.

  • Q4 Gross Loss Ratio — defined as the ratio of Losses and Loss Adjustment Expense to Gross Earned Premium — was 63%, down 14pp YoY.
  • Q4 Net Loss Ratio — defined as the ratio of Losses and Loss Adjustment Expense, less amounts ceded to reinsurers, to Net Earned Premium — was 62%, down 16pp YoY.

For context, large insurers have loss ratios in the range of 60% to 80% so that puts Lemonade as one of the top performers in the industry. Moreover, Lemonade's loss ratios are still on a downward trajectory, which means that Lemonade's underwriting models are getting smarter and more efficient, and over time, I see the potential for its loss ratios to fall below 60%, thus significantly outperforming even the largest and most experienced insurers in the game. This will further solidify Lemonade's cost advantage moat.

So how was Lemonade able to consistently improve its loss ratios over the last few quarters? According to management, the following factors have contributed to improving loss ratios:

  • Diversification: geographic and product diversification helps Lemonade manage CAT exposure, thus minimizing losses.
  • Not renewing certain policies: management has deliberately taken steps to stop renewing certain CAT-exposed homeowners policies that do not meet its AI-powered underwriting standards. While this reduces Lemonade's IFP growth, it significantly improves Lemonade's loss ratios.
  • Newer verticals maturing: At the beginning, newer verticals tend to exhibit higher loss ratios, but as time goes by, loss ratios from these verticals will eventually fall. Take the pet vertical (launched in 2020) for example. As you can see below, as Lemonade Pet IFP increases, its Gross Loss Ratio has gone nowhere but down, from 98% in 2021 to just 69% in 2024.

The improvements in Lemonade's unit economics reflect the scalability of its business model and its potential to drive serious earnings in the coming decades. This is even more impressive considering that Lemonade offers the lowest price in the industry. Over time, Lemonade's digital business architecture will drive accelerating returns while traditional insurers struggle with diminishing returns.

But first, Lemonade needs to scale.

Management understands this, which is why they have ramped up spending to drive further growth — more specifically, through a growth funding program called Synthetic Agents.

Launched in July 2023, the Synthetic Agents program is "a novel financial structure that unlocks growth without depleting cash". In essence, Lemonade has partnered with leading venture firm General Catalyst (GC) to fund 80% of Lemonade's Sales and Marketing Growth spend, and in return, Lemonade will pay back the principal borrowed plus a commission of up to 16% of the premiums GC helped to finance. Once fully paid, Lemonade will retain all future income from the customers acquired from the Synthetic Agents program.

This program has a couple of benefits for Lemonade. It allows Lemonade to accelerate growth without:

  • depleting cash levels, thus protecting its balance sheet, and
  • issuing more equity, thus preventing shareholder dilution.

Initially, GC will finance up to $150M of Lemonade's growth efforts, but this deal has since been extended twice for another $140M in January 2024 and an additional $200M in its recent Q4 report. Safe to say, both parties are happy with the outcome of the program.

Keep in mind that the Growth spend funded by GC will be reflected in Lemonade's income statement, under Sales & Marketing Expenses, which will be dilutive to Net Income. On the other hand, it will also be reflected on the cash flow statement, under Proceeds from Borrowings Under Financing Agreement, which will be accretive to Financing Cash Flow, and thus, Cash levels.

With that said, as a result of the Synthetic Agents program, Lemonade's Sales & Marketing Expenses spiked 95% YoY to $48M. Operating Expenses excluding Loss and Loss Adjustment Expense, Net, was $124M, up 28% YoY. As a reminder, IFP grew 26% YoY. In other words, Lemonade's topline is growing slower than its expense line, which is not good.

However, if we remove Growth spend (mainly funding from GC) from the equation, we can see that Lemonade is driving considerable IFP growth while Operating Expenses excluding Growth spend have barely changed. This is an extreme display of operating leverage.

I understand what management is trying to convey: Lemonade has a scalable business model that doesn't require a marginal increase in fixed costs. But in my view, not including Growth spend in Operating Expenses is misleading because growth would have been much lower without the Synthetic Agents program.

Growth spend, whether borrowed or not, is still an expense, and therefore, it still needs to be taken into consideration in the company’s profitability analysis. With this in mind, I prefer to analyze Lemonade's financials by including the Growth spend funded by GC.

Moving down the income statement, Adjusted EBITDA was $(24)M in Q4. If we exclude the $36M of Growth spend in Q4, Lemonade would have turned Adjusted EBITDA positive for the first time ever — but again, it's a disservice to exclude Growth spend from the profit equation.

Regardless, we can see that Lemonade is driving significant operating leverage, with Adjusted EBITDA Margins improving 9pp YoY and 20pp QoQ, to (16)% as of Q4.

We can see the same thing for Net Income as well, which was $(30)M, with Margins improving 17pp YoY and 30pp QoQ to (20)%.

But on a less positive note, expect profitability to take a slight hit in Q1, mainly due to the California Wildfires. In Q1, management expects Adjusted EBITDA of $(49)M to $(46)M, implying an Adjusted EBITDA Margin of (33)%.

This guidance includes a ~$20M impact from the wildfires. This figure might look large, but it could've been larger if not for Lemonade's reinsurance contracts — gross losses from the wildfires were estimated to be $45M but only $20M will impact the bottom line as a portion of those losses will be ceded to reinsurers.

For the full year, management expects Adjusted EBITDA of $(140)M to $(135)M, implying a (21)% Margin, so expect margins to improve after Q1 and beyond.

This guidance also includes approximately $165M of Growth spend in 2025, so technically, Adjusted EBITDA would have been positive without this Growth spend.

That said, despite the California Wildfires and a 40% YoY increase in Growth spend, Lemonade is expected to continue to deliver operational efficiencies in 2025, eventually exiting 2026 with positive Adjusted EBITDA.

Keep in mind that Lemonade achieved all these profitability improvements despite one of the most challenging macroeconomic conditions, characterized by elevated inflation and numerous catastrophes.

For instance, over the last 45 years, 2023 (purple line) and 2024 (orange line) were the two years with the most number of individual weather and climate disasters in the US with at least $1B in damages, with 28 and 27 events, respectively. To put this into perspective, the 1980-2023 annual average (black line) is just 9 events.

And yet, Lemonade's margins continue to improve. Sure, there will be dips in profits along the way (as exemplified by the wildfires in Q1), but over a long period, Lemonade continues to demonstrate strong operating leverage.

This speaks volumes about the durability, stability, and scalability of Lemonade's business model.

  • Durability to withstand catastrophes.
  • Stability to minimize margin volatility.
  • Scalability to improve earnings.

Back in 2021, I was skeptical about Lemonade's business model and its ability to turn a profit. However, its recent performance proves otherwise.

Lemonade's profitability metrics are getting sweeter by the day.

Through the combination of a delightful brand, killer prices, highly-recurring Revenue streams, a reinsurance construct, and an ever-improving AI, Lemonade is able to scale its business rapidly while driving loss ratios down — altogether, they form Lemonade's secret recipe for long-term profitable growth.

Health: Cash Flow Positivity Marks a New Era

As of Q4, Lemonade has $1.0B of Cash and Investments:

  • Cash and Cash Equivalents were $376M. As you can see below, this figure has been expanding over the last few quarters.
  • Total Investments were $635M, consisting primarily of fixed maturity securities that generate Lemonade's Net Investment Income.

Factoring in Total Debt of $107M, Lemonade has a Net Cash position of $904M. To put this into perspective, Lemonade's Net Cash position represents nearly 50% of its current Market Cap of $2.0B!

Lemonade also maintains a risk-based capital ratio of more than 500%, which is well above risk-based capital standards and other minimum capital and surplus requirements — a ratio of more than 200% implies "an acceptably low expectation of becoming financially impaired". Anything below this threshold would trigger some level of regulatory intervention.

The lower the percentage, the more severe the regulatory response, including, in the event of a mandatory control level event (total adjusted capital falls below 70% of the insurer's authorized control level risk-based capital), placing the insurance company into receivership. As of December 31, 2024, our risk-based capital ratio was 577% for LIC and 525% for MIC.

(Lemonade FY2024 10-K)

That said, I expect Lemonade's Net Cash position to expand over time, as Lemonade has just turned Free Cash Flow positive. In Q4, FCF was $11M, representing a FCF Margin of 7%, which improved 32pp YoY. As you can see, this is the second straight quarter of positive FCF.

Management also shared Adjusted FCF, which adds back Net Borrowings under the Synthetic Agents program. In Q4, Adjusted FCF was $27M, representing an 18% Margin, which improved 28pp YoY. For the full year, 2024 Adjusted FCF was $48M. Moving forward, management expects their second consecutive year of positive Adjusted FCF in 2025.

Again, I'm not a big fan of adding back borrowed money from the Synthetic Agents program as:

  1. FCF would have been much lower without growth funding from GC.
  2. It is still an expense that will eventually be paid back to GC.

The important thing here is that Lemonade has turned FCF positive, a strong signal of a viable business model with ongoing operating leverage.

In the first nine years as a company, Lemonade has financed its operations mainly through private and public equity offerings, diluting shareholders in the process.

Fundamentally, being FCF-positive marks a new era of true profit generation and shareholder value creation. In the long run, incremental FCF/share growth will be accretive to the company's valuation.

Valuation: Sip or Skip?

Let me preface this section by saying that it is challenging to value Lemonade stock, given the company's small size and unprofitability. As such, I'll be using a few methods to gauge the company's valuation and how attractive it is from a risk-to-reward standpoint.

First, let's look at valuation multiples.

As it stands, Lemonade trades at a Price to Sales multiple of 3.7x, which is a significant discount to its peak multiple of 107x. It is also trading close to its bottom multiple of 1.8x. So from a historical standpoint, Lemonade stock looks very cheap.

We can see the same thing for its Price to Book multiple — a more traditional metric to value insurance companies — which is at about 3.4x as of this writing. Again, the ratio is well below its peak multiple of 19x, implying relative undervaluation.

If we compare its multiple to some of its larger peers, we can see that Lemonade is trading at a premium to its peer group. Aside from Progressive (PGR), Lemonade is trading at a premium — this is justified given Lemonade's higher growth rate and growth potential. After all, these are the companies that Lemonade is currently disrupting.

On the other hand, Lemonade is trading at a significant premium to other insurtech companies, some of which have much higher growth rates than Lemonade. Therefore, Lemonade may not offer the best value if you want exposure to insurtech companies.

On a side note, institutions have been loading up on Lemonade stock, which may offer some validation for investors.

On the other side, insider ownership is particularly low for a small-cap growth company, with both co-founders still leading the company. As of this writing, CEO Daniel Schreiber owns only 0.07% of the company, while CTO Shai Wininger owns about 4.65%. Collectively, insiders own 5.48% of Lemonade stock — not exactly a high enough figure that reflects skin in the game.

I've also done a DCF analysis to determine Lemonade's intrinsic value per share, but keep in mind that I'm taking an unconventional approach here — I'll be using Adjusted EBITDA as a proxy for Cash Flow since management did not provide their long-term FCF Margin target.

During Lemonade's Investor Day, management provided the following "simplified approach to valuation", highlighting that they expect the company to grow Gross Earned Premium by 30% annually for the next 7 years. They also applied a 12% Adjusted EBITDA Margin against Gross Earned Premium, which, I assume, would be their long-term margin target.

As a result, management arrived at an intrinsic value per share of $90+.

Now, management has guided for a $1B Gross Earned Premium in 2025. Assuming Lemonade has reached optimal margins today, a 12% Margin will get us an Adjusted EBITDA of $120M.

Management also guided for $656M of Revenue in 2025 — an Adjusted EBITDA of $120M translates to an Adjusted EBITDA Margin as a % of Revenue of about 18%. This will be Lemonade's long-term Adjusted EBITDA Margin in my DCF model.

With that being said, here are my key assumptions:

  • FY2034 Revenue of $3.6B. I have used consensus estimates for the first three years. After that, I expect growth to decelerate to just 15% by 2032. Remember that management expects Gross Earned Premium to grow at a CAGR of 30% for the next 7 years, so my assumption is actually very conservative.
  • FY2034 Adjusted EBITDA Margin of 18%, as explained earlier. Per management, they expect Adjusted EBITDA to turn positive by the end of 2026, so I'm projecting positive margins after 2026, eventually reaching an optimal Adjusted EBITDA Margin of 18%.
  • FY2034 "Cash Flow" of $512M at a 14% Margin. The formula for "Cash Flow" is Adjusted EBITDA minus Income Tax and Capital Expenditures.

Based on a Perpetual Growth Rate of 3% and a Discount Rate of 12%, I get an intrinsic value per share of $50 for Lemonade stock, which is nearly double its current price of $28. This is the base case.

Again, I'm using a simplified and unconventional DCF method, so take my valuation estimate with a grain of salt.

Below, I've also provided my bear and bull cases.

As a reference, analysts have an average price target of $32 a share, with a street-low of $15 and a street-high of $60.

Be that as it may, despite being down 85% from its all-time highs, Lemonade, as a business, is stronger than it has ever been. Compared to a few years ago, Lemonade is a much larger business with: 1) more product verticals, 2) accelerating growth, 3) loss ratios below its target range of 70%, 4) a clear path towards GAAP profitability, and 5) cash flow positivity.

What's more, following the recent 50% selloff, Lemonade now trades near the bottom of its valuation multiple range, with nearly 50% of its Market Cap being Net Cash. Alongside improving fundamentals, I think Lemonade stock offers decent upside potential with a good margin of safety.

Risks

  • Competition: Insurance is a highly competitive space. This includes more established companies like GEICO, Progressive, and State Farm, which have strong brand reputations, higher financial ratings, and better bundling options. In addition, the rise of autonomous vehicles and robotaxis may disrupt the car insurance industry, thus jeopardizing Lemonade's Car business.
  • Catastrophes: Insurance companies experience significant fluctuations in their operating results due to natural disasters. While Lemonade's reinsurance contracts soften its losses from any particular catastrophic event, they do not completely negate the losses incurred. As such, Lemonade's business fundamentals and stock price will likely fluctuate as unpredictably as the weather.
  • Unprofitability: As discussed earlier, Lemonade is still unprofitable on a GAAP basis, which may continue to pressure the stock until the company turns a profit. This is especially true in this uncertain market environment where investors are likely to engage in a flight to quality, moving their assets to more profitable, stable, and defensive investments.
  • Tariffs: As you know, we are currently in the middle of a tariff war, which indirectly impacts the insurance industry. For instance, tariffs raise the price of imported goods such as timber and cars, which lead to higher claims costs for insurers, thus lowering overall profitability. While I believe higher tariffs will benefit Lemonade as more customers look for cheaper options, increased claims costs as a result of these tariffs could further delay Lemonade's profitability goals.
  • High Short Interest: Lemonade has a Short Ratio of more than 20%, so skepticism is quite high. While a high Short Ratio sets the stage for a possible short squeeze, it could point to underlying concerns about the company’s fundamentals — in Lemonade's case, it's probably unprofitability.

Thesis

Lemonade is redefining insurance, disrupting a centuries-old industry by leveraging reinsurance, AI, and killer prices.

Over the last decade, Lemonade has built itself strong competitive moats — brand, cost advantage, and switching costs — that position the company well for robust Revenue and earnings growth for many years to come.

Compared to a few years ago, IFP has more than quadrupled, loss ratios have come down materially, and FCF has turned positive. Despite the improvement in business fundamentals, Lemonade stock is still down 85% from its peak and 50% from its IPO price.

At 50% Net Cash and trading at about 3x its sales and book value, Lemonade stock offers decent value for investors who want exposure to a disruptive insurtech company.

As I write this, the stock market is going through a major sell-off — and Lemonade has not been spared. Perhaps, this could be a good time to take a sip of Lemonade.

data.title: Dominion Energy: A Bet On Growing Energy Needs
data.description:
Introduction

Dominion Energy (NYSE: NYSE:D) is one of the largest energy companies in the US, providing electricity to millions across Virginia, North/South Carolina, and natural gas to various states. Its current portfolio has more than 30,000 megawatts of electrical generation and more than 90,000 miles of electric transmission/distribution lines. It can generate electricity from various sources, such as nuclear, hydroelectric, and natural gas.

I believe that Dominion can be a reliable investment, especially now with the current volatility in the markets and the rise of energy demands in Virginia, North Carolina, and South Carolina, thanks to current migration trends and the rise of energy-hungry data centers.

Segment Overview

Dominion Energy splits its business between 3 distinct segments, with two of them representing geographical importance.

Dominion Energy Virginia, or DEV, incorporates its electric utility operation in Virginia and some parts of North Carolina, which serves around 2.7 million customers with various demand needs. It has the most significant portion of electric generating capacity with around 23,800 megawatts which generated $10.2B in revenue in 2024. It also has major expansion plans, such as the Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind project, that would add 2.6 gigawatts and boost its clean energy production.

Dominion Energy South Carolina combines both its electric and its remaining gas distribution utilities in the state, which provides electricity to around 800,000 customers and nat gas to around 500,000. It contributes a mix of about 5,500 megawatts of generation capacity with a growing solar power segment and has been a growing segment since the 2019 SCANA acquisition. DESC generated $3.3B in sales in 2024.

Contracted Energy manages non-regulated energy assets, which are mainly renewables under long-term contracts. The assets include solar and wind facilities, but also merchant generation assets like the Millstone Power Station. It accounts for a smaller but growing portion of revenue with around 4,500 megawatts of capacity. The segment shows how Dominion can leverage power purchase agreements with large clients such as data centers and their huge power needs.

Market Dynamics and Looking Ahead

If we directly look at the momentum from rising energy needs, particularly from data centers, Dominion is in a prime position to capitalize on its infrastructure strengths and its regulated reliability. Data centers require reliable, scalable, and increasingly sustainable energy, which Dominion can certainly deliver on. Its core market, Virginia, has become a hub for these data centers, with Dominion expecting a 5-7% annual load growth through 2035, far outgrowing historical utility norms. The Northern Virginia data center market is larger than the next five US markets combined and larger than the next four world markets combined, which makes Dominion prone to capitalize, especially with its $50B capital investment plan. The current migration dynamics in both North and South Carolina will also boost the user base of Dominion Energy.

Dominion does a fairly good job in shifting toward renewables and net-zero goals, with its share of coal energy declining. Its peers also pushed for clean energy, intensifying competition. Dominion also relies on natural gas, which could expose the company to price volatility, though its regulated status can allow fuel cost pass-throughs to customers

Looking at its peers, Duke and Southern compete in the Southeast with similar regulated models, although Dominion has a strong leadership in wind energy generation, giving it an edge in renewables over most of its peers outside of NextEra. NextEra dominates merchant markets with its huge presence in renewables but lacks Dominion’s regulated stability. Although independent power producers and giant tech firms are entering into power agreements that can challenge Dominion’s contracted segment, its vast scale and reliability leave the utility company with a strong competitive advantage. The Inflation Reduction Act also boosts Dominion’s renewable energy edge with access to tax credits.

Dominion also recently stated that it plans on raising consumer bills by 14% to cover costs linked to soaring demand. This would impact customers from Virginia as well as Loudoun County, which has the largest concentration of data centers in the world. Dominion also wants data centers to make 14-year commitments to pay for their requested power even if they use less energy. Thanks to these measures, the company expects its 2025 operating EPS to range from $3.28 to $3.52, which would amount to a tremendous growth of 18-27% YoY.

Valuation

With the recent market sell-offs, Dominion has been shedding its valuation, offering a slight discount for its relatively strong tariffs dependency free business. P/E Non-GAAP stands at 19.11 while its FWD P/E is currently at 15.54, thanks to its potential in 2025. EV/EBITDA reaches 12.6 and P/B 1.71. I would not say that these metrics are cheap at all, but are more representative of the future prospects for Dominion and its near monopoly. Investors are paying for stability and relative clean energy upside.

Dominion has lower returns compared to its peers due to its stability and its focus on regulated markets, compared to NextEra, for example, and its high-growth model. I believe that these have potential upsides with the maturity of its CapEx plan and scaling its dependence on large 14-years contracts with data centers.

And while waiting for Dominion to boost its returns, and its CapEx plan to pan out, the company is offering a generous dividend yield of 5.06%. I would say that growth has been poor for its yield as the company focuses on expanding its offerings in its area of operation.

Conclusion

I believe that Dominion is a buy based on the stability of its business, which can certainly weather the current tumult in the market and does not suffer from the tariffs imposed by the Trump administration. The company has great foundations to significantly outperform normal utility growth.

data.title: You're Unlikely To Get Alphabet Cheaper Than This
data.description:
My Thesis Update

This year became a major challenge for every tech company out there, and although Alphabet Inc. (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (TSX:GOOG:CA) is listed as a Communications Services firm, its stock price suffered like any other mega-cap representative by losing over $500 billion in market cap on a YTD basis (-22%). So my bullish rating on GOOG/GOOGL, while it was quite successful at first, proved premature - especially the latest update:

Despite that ongoing negative sentiment and correction on the stock market, I think investors shouldn't overreact - Google is currently quite cheap, and I believe it's unlikely to buy it cheaper in the next few years. It wasn't overvalued before the correction took off, and it's currently carrying a massive margin of safety while being impacted by the China-US war in a limited way. That's why I reiterate my "Buy" rating on GOOG stock today.

My Updated Thesis

Google presented its Q4 and FY2024 results in early February, with full-year revenues hitting $350 billion (+14% YoY), almost in line with the consensus estimates, and EPS of ~$8.04 (+38.62% YoY), which was ~0.7% above expectations. During Q4, the firm's revenue of $96.5 billion (+12 YoY) led to an operating profit of $31 billion (+31% YoY, with a margin of 32%), thanks to the overall strength in both Google Search and Google Cloud segments. For example, the core of Alphabet's business - their Google Services unit - saw a 10% YoY increase in revenues with an 11% increase coming from ads revenue, which doesn't indicate any weakness in the search business if you ask me. At the same time, YouTube advertising revenues remain impressive, growing at double digits YoY growth rates (+14% YoY in Q4) thanks to "brand advertising and U.S. election spending" (that was a double vs 2020 cycle).

It has been the main fear of investors and skeptics that GPT models and various chatbots based on them will replace Google search shortly - it's not happening just yet as the search and other ads revenue experience a "broad-based strength led by the financial services vertical (particularly insurance) and retail", according to the firm's CBO (taken from the earnings call). Especially the fears look unjustified once we take a look at Google Services' margins dynamics: EBIT margin went up from 35% to 39%, which is quite impressive.

Google Cloud, although it's currently just ~12.4% of consolidated sales, outgrows the over segments and its industry in general - the management noted on the call that GCP grew "at a rate that was much higher than cloud overall" with a 30% YoY revenue expansion in Q4 and operating income (~$2.1 billion) seeing its margin doubling from 9.4% to 17.5%. Compared to Amazon's (AMZN) AWS EBIT margin of 10.75% that's a great achievement, in my opinion, that says a lot about GCP's AI infrastructure potential in the next few years. It's already growing strongly, and given the ever-rising need for cheap and reliable LLM models that can be used not just for chatting, but for production-ready apps, I think GCP is going to become a first go-to solution for developers with its existing ecosystem and lower-than-peers costs per million tokens usage.

Of course, it's impossible to retain the lion's share of the search market without incurring costs, so Google's aggressive push into AI and the need to support burgeoning demand comes at a cost. The management guided for ~$75 billion in CapEx for 2025, exceeding most banks' prior estimates (based on Goldman Sachs commentary, proprietary source). They want to "alleviate the capacity constraints" in the Cloud and the CAPEX primarily targets technical infrastructure (servers, data centers, and networking), likely leading to higher depreciation growth in FY2025 and putting pressure on the P&L, following a 28% depreciation increase in FY2024. Investors and analysts usually don't like this kind of P&L pressure, and so we logically see a continuation of the Street's negative revisions:

At the beginning of calendar 2022, we can see that the consensus expected Google's EPS for FY2026 to arrive at $11.43, now it's $10.21 (a 10.7% cut over the past 3 years). I think we'll likely see another round of negative revisions in the next months, thanks to the rising odds of recession in the US. But I don't think the magnitude of those likely revisions will be over 5% (regarding FY2026).

So if my assumption is correct, then Google's EPS will grow by 8.25% YoY instead of the current consensus figure of 13.86%. In both cases, we see an expansion, but the market is pricing Google as if it's already in recession mode (or at least as if it's nearing the lows of late 2022):

Let's make a bolder assumption: Google's consensus EPS for FY2026 will take a 10% downward revision instead of the 5% I assume as a base case scenario. This way, the absolute EPS figure will arrive at $9.12, which will be a 2.55% YoY expansion (vs. the 13.86% YoY growth currently expected). History hints that in such cases we usually see some stability in the P/E ratio, as the stock price correction that preceded the actual EPS deterioration was a pricing-in event. That's why I wouldn't assume a further fall in Google's price-to-earnings multiple - it's likely to stay in its historical norm ranges.

So at 18x earnings and with an EPS of $9.12 for FY2026, Google's fair value is ~$164.16 per share. That's already an 11.1% upside from today's stock price, and this price target assumes a severe deterioration in today's consensus figures. If the current consensus is revised downward by 5% instead of 10% from here, we'll see a fair value per share of ~$174.6, which is over 18% above today's price. So I think Google stock is now unlikely to go down much lower from here. Yes, there's a risk of another leg lower, but if you're willing to hold for more than 1–3 years, you're unlikely to buy it much cheaper than that.

Risks To Consider

I should admit that investing in Google stock comes with a lot of risks. First, as I noted in my previous article, there's "extreme regulatory pressure and risk" from ongoing antitrust investigations, lawsuits, and new legislation, particularly in the EU and US, which may lead to higher expenses on fines, new policies that may restrict Google's activities in particular markets (and hence cut its growth potential there), etc. Second, I may be underestimating the longevity of today's lower-than-historical valuation multiples for the stock. I mean, if GOOG had been trading above 20-25x earnings before the correction took off, it doesn't necessarily mean that it should return to that range. If the market's turmoil continues, we may see another "normality" for Google at 10-15x earnings - this way, my assumption of "margin of safety" will become premature.

Your Takeaway

Despite the existing risk factors, I wouldn't write off Google stock's recovery prospects in the next few years, and surely, I don't foresee a lost decade for the firm (although there are some analysts who think that Google will lose its dominance on the search market shortly).

While everyone is afraid of the US-China tariffs and economic tensions, Google is punished like any other stock out there, although its search market share, there is very limited and underexposed to China [and South Asia in general]. The market hit that followed from tariff announcements is unfairly impacting Google's stock price, in my view, and hence creating a great opportunity to double down or initiate a brand-new position in this steady grower.

I like what I see in their GCP Platform, the whole ecosystem, and its competitive edge compared to others not just in key metrics like cost per million tokens, but in the level of infrastructure development. I believe that as their revenue structure keeps shifting to the Cloud's side, the business diversification created will help them mitigate most of the risks related to the potential search market loss in the next few years. That's why I think that the stock's valuation should eventually get back to the historical range, and if so, today's stock price might mark a cyclical bottom. I reiterate my "Buy" on Google.

Good luck with your investments!

Investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that when shares are redeemed, they may be worth more or less than original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Load performance reflects maximum sales charges or contingent deferred sales charges (CDSC) as applicable. A Class has a maximum sales charge of 4.00%

The referenced fund is offered in multiple share classes. Please read the prospectus for information on fees, expenses and holding periods that may apply to each class.

  • Inception date of benchmark return is that of the fund’s oldest share class.
  • The Net Expense Ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver by the Adviser through February 1, 2025, to limit the ordinary operating expenses of the fund.
  • The Adjusted Expense Ratio (“Adjusted”) is based on the Net Expense Ratio, but excludes certain investment expenses, such as interest expense from borrowings and repurchase agreements and dividend expense from investments on short sales.
  • Benchmark-Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index.

Unless otherwise noted, data is as of 12.31.2024. Data subject to change. Partial year returns are cumulative. Returns reflect the reinvestment of dividends.

The referenced index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment. Index performance does not reflect transaction costs, fees, or expenses.

Index data source: FundStation.

Performance Review

The Fund (Institutional Class) finished the fourth quarter down 2.39 percent (net), outperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, by 0.67 percent. This brought the net 2024 calendar year absolute and relative performances to 3.16 percent and 1.91 percent, respectively. Outperformance was driven by sector and security selection and the Fund’s yield advantage over the benchmark. Duration detracted from absolute returns as interest rates rose following stronger-than-expected economic data and in reaction to the U.S. election results. The Fund benefitted from an overweight allocation to structured credit as the sector broadly outperformed other fixed-income sectors such as corporate credit due to more pronounced spread tightening.

Selection within investment-grade (IG) corporates and Agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) were key contributors to relative performance.

Strategy and Positioning

The Fund maintained a relatively neutral overall risk positioning, reflecting a balanced approach focused on capital preservation while selectively capitalizing on opportunities across various higher quality credit segments. Positioning favors higher credit quality forms of carry, shorter duration instruments, such as senior tranches within non-Agency RMBS, esoteric asset-backed securities (ABS), and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs). The Fund maintained a slight duration overweight versus the benchmark. The overweight is focused on the front to intermediate part of the curve, where yields are elevated by historical standards and most prone to fall should monetary policy normalize and the U.S. Treasury curve bull steepen.

The Fund diversified its duration exposure by adding Treasury Inflation Protected Securities (TIPS) to protect against rising inflation. The Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) allocation increased by 3 percent during the quarter, with exposure ending at approximately 23 percent of the Fund. The allocation is concentrated in near-production coupon mortgage pools which possess high carry profiles and wide nominal spreads due to heightened interest rate volatility.

The Fund is underweight investment-grade corporates where spreads are compressed. An active primary market offered some opportunities to add marginally to the sector at attractive valuations. The Fund’s allocation to Treasurys remains higher than its historic norm of 16 percent, given the combination of historically high yields and compressing risk premia.

QTD Contributors / Detractors

Carry contributed 1.5 percent to absolute return and 0.2 percent to relative return. Duration detracted by 4.6 percent on an absolute basis and 0.2 percent on a relative basis. IG corporates’ spread tightening contributed on an absolute and relative basis, as did tightening in the small allocation to high-yield corporates. Spread tightening across structured credit contributed on an absolute and relative basis.

Economic and Market Review

The U.S. economy has good momentum heading into 2025, but the policy outlook from Washington elevates uncertainty. Recent economic data have been solid, with fourth quarter real gross domestic product (GDP) on track for about 2 percent annualized growth. The outlook for consumer spending remains positive, supported by healthy growth in inflation-adjusted labor income and a wealth effect driven by rising asset prices. Financial conditions have also turned more supportive as credit growth is reaccelerating and optimism about artificial intelligence induces a positive outlook for capex. Disinflationary progress has stalled a bit in recent months, but fundamentals point to a further slowdown in inflation as wage pressures and housing inflation ease further. With a new administration in office, investors want to know how the evolving policy landscape will alter the outlook. In the immediate future, we expect a boost to both consumer and business sentiment, aided by expectations of deregulation and further tax cuts. Post-election surveys have already shown increased optimism about the outlook, which could support consumption, investment, and hiring in coming months. Looking beyond the immediate sentiment boost, the outlook becomes more uncertain and depends on the ultimate policy mix of the new administration. Extension of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) would prevent a fiscal drag, but we see limited scope for new tax cuts as the fiscal backdrop has worsened. Broad tariffs could weigh on growth and push up prices, though we expect more targeted tariffs will ultimately be used.

All together, we see moderate growth in the U.S. economy in 2025 as these policy shifts play out. Economic fundamentals remain solid, with strong household and corporate balance sheets. The Federal Reserve (Fed) will likely ease policy further toward a neutral setting, but tariffs could slow the pace of rate cuts by interrupting the disinflationary trend. The U.S. economy should remain a global outperformer, though we expect continued bifurcation across sectors, particularly as new policies begin to have an impact.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Please see page 2 for additional performance information.

Bps (basis points): One basis point is equal to 0.01%. Carry: The difference between the cost of financing an asset and the interest received on that asset. Dry powder: highly liquid assets, such as cash or money market instruments, that can be invested when more attractive investment opportunities arise. Duration: A measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond to a change in interest rates. In general, the higher the duration the more the bond’s price will change with interest rate movements. Spread: Spread refers to the difference between yields on different debt instruments and is used to understand the relative value and risks of different debt instruments.

Risk Considerations. This fund may not be suitable for all investors.

In general, the value of a fixed-income security falls when interest rates rise and rises when interest rates fall. Longer term bonds are more sensitive to interest rate changes and subject to greater volatility than those with shorter maturities. During periods of declining rates, the interest rates on floating rate securities generally reset downward and their value is unlikely to rise to the same extent as comparable fixed rate securities.

Investors in asset-backed securities, including collateralized loan obligations (“CLOs”), generally receive payments that are part interest and part return of principal. These payments may vary based on the rate loans are repaid. Some asset-backed securities may have structures that make their reaction to interest rates and other factors difficult to predict, making their prices volatile and they are subject to liquidity and valuation risk. CLOs bear similar risks to investing in loans directly.

Investments in loans involve special types of risks, including credit, interest rate, counterparty, prepayment, liquidity, and valuation risks. Loans are often below investment grade, may be unrated, and typically offer a fixed or floating interest rate.

High yield and unrated debt securities are at a greater risk of default than investment grade bonds and may be less liquid, which may increase volatility.

The fund’s use of leverage, through borrowings or instruments such as derivatives, may cause the fund to be more volatile and riskier than if it had not been leveraged. The more a fund invests in leveraged instruments, the more the leverage will magnify any gains or losses on those investments.

Investments in reverse repurchase agreements expose the fund to many of the same risks as leveraged instruments, such as derivatives.

You may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares.

Please read the prospectus for more detailed information regarding these and other risks.

Index Definition. Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based flagship benchmark that measures the investment grade, U.S. dollar- denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM pass-throughs), ABS, and CMBS (agency and non-agency). The Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Index measures the market for USD- denominated non-investment grade, fixed rate, taxable corporate bonds. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody's, Fitch, and S&P is Bal/BB+/ BB+ or below. The index excludes emerging market debt.

data.title: Verizon: Not Overly Incentivized To Stay Bullish Ahead Of Q1 Earnings
data.description:
A Steady Start To The Year

While it’s still early days, 2025 so far, is proving to be a fairly decent year for the shareholders of the telecommunications giant- Verizon Communications Inc. (NYSE:VZ). While the large-cap equity benchmark has collapsed by 14%, and other telecommunication stocks have fallen by a lesser threshold (on average). VZ has bucked the trend and eked out positive gains of over 6% on a YTD basis.

Admittedly, a lot can change over a two-week time frame (more so in the current environment with the VIX behaving the way it is), but we suspect VZ’s ability to keep up this bout of alpha, will be more keenly tested on the 22nd of April (exactly two weeks from today) when it releases its Q1 earnings.

If you’re contemplating a position in VZ ahead of this event, here are some of the important sub-plots to consider.

Earnings Expectations

Given VZ’s recent track record in meeting street estimates, we suspect stakeholders of Verizon won’t necessarily be in the most intrepid state. To be clear, the uncertainty isn’t linked to the bottom-line performance, where VZ has met or beaten estimates for the last 10 consecutive quarters (equating to an average EPS beat of 1.4% per quarter). Rather, it’s because of the topline track record, where VZ has been rather inconsistent, beating estimates only 50% of the time over the last 10 quarters. All in all, VZ is a company that has managed an average revenue miss of -0.2% per quarter, so if it can do better than this in Q1, it should be taken well by the market.

For the upcoming Q1, the two key headline metrics to track are a revenue figure of $33.3B, and a normalized EPS of $1.15.

Another reason not to get too gung-ho about VZ is because Q1 is their seasonally weakest quarter (if VZ meets consensus on the topline, it would still represent growth of less than 1% YoY, lower than +1.6% growth seen in Q4-24), and this year, the pressure has been amplified by a generally weak market (as opposed to 9m p.a. net postpaid additions seen over the last 3 years, this year, the industry as a whole, is only likely to see net additions to the tune of 8-8.5m (includes both customers and businesses), which would be around 8% lower than last year), and “ aggressive” competitive promotional activity by the likes of AT&T (T) and T-Mobile US, Inc (TMUS) since the turn of 2025. VZ not only abandoned the promotional game at the end of the holiday season, but also resorted to further price-ups in January, on the back of price-ups in the latter half of 2024 ( 4 in total last year). Because of all this, churn is likely to be elevated ( 3-5bps YoY) and total postpaid net adds are unlikely to grow, after a very strong Q4 where they saw 568K of net adds (note also that traditionally VZ has witnessed net losses as opposed to net additions).

VZ management can help ameliorate the likely weak subscriber additions of Q1, by talking up its prospects for the rest of the year (expect things like their perk heavy myPlan, and growing personalization efforts for customers to be highlighted). It will also be interesting to see if they can keep growing their consumer ARPA at over 4% because of these price increases (4.2% in Q4)

To be fair to VZ, their recent pricing actions should still add about $1B of incremental revenue this year, and wireless services revenue growth for the FY is still expected to come in at respectable levels of 2-2.8% YoY (although still lower than the 3.1% YoY growth seen in FY24). Also, the level of device upgrades this year is expected to be much better than last year, which was one of the weakest in its recent history. Average device lives have now crossed the 40-month landmark and will need to be replaced, although we suspect most of the upgrade benefits will take place in H2 as opposed to Q1 (for the whole year, VZ is budgeting for a mid-single-digit increase on upgrades).

VZ’s ability to generate decent free cash flow too will be scrutinized, given its humungous debt servicing burden ($144B of total debt, with 82% of this unsecured, equating to 2.3x VZ’s EBITDA) and dividend commitments (it has been growing its dividends annually for 18 years on the trot). Once again, don’t expect Q1 to be particularly resplendent on the operating cash flow front, but even for the FY, the FCF outlook relative to last year, isn’t the greatest. Last year’s FCF of close to $20B, did benefit from one-time effects to the tune of $1.4B on a net basis (positive contribution of $2b from the Vertical bridge tower transaction and negative contribution of $0.6B related to severance payments) which won’t quite be there this year. All in all, the FY25 FCF is expected to dip by around 9% to $18bn (mid-point of guidance), even as CAPEX commitments (in high-speed fiber, AI, wireless networks, etc.) step up by around $1bn YoY this year to $18bn (mid-point).

What does this mean for the dividend and the debt burden? Well, assuming they don’t buy back any shares (they intend to resume buybacks only when debt to EBITDA drops to below 2.25x), and carry on with their usual 2% dividend hike p.a., they could be looking at a cash dividend outflow of $11.5bn for the year, which their expected FCF (of $18bn) should cover by 1.6x. VZ could still have $6.5bn which it can then use to bring down its debt, although this will still only be a pittance (they’ll only be bringing down less than 5% of their total debt).

Closing Thought - Is Verizon A Good Buy Ahead of The Q1 Results?

As suggested earlier, Q1-25 is unlikely to be the strongest quarter for VZ, and the charting and valuation backdrop too isn’t the most attractive either.

Verizon’s weekly price movements (seen in the image below) over the last two years, appear to be taking the firm of a rising wedge pattern, with two converging trendlines pointing to some maturation in the uptrend as buying momentum wanes. For the uninitiated, the rising wedge typically points to the risk of a reversal.

We also acknowledge VZ’s current dividend yield of over 6% is still quite attractive, but it is not hugely better than what other integrated telecommunication services stocks across the world currently offer (industry average of 6.2%).

Meanwhile, the relative strength charts using the telecommunications universe at the base helps identify suitable rotational opportunities within this terrain. As opposed to conditions earlier in the year, VZ’s relative strength ratio is now no longer trading well below its mean, but around 7% higher, dampening the incentive for investors to rotate into this.

At current price levels, VZ’s forward valuations are also not exactly cheap either. As opposed to its rolling P/E average of 8.4x over the last 5 years, the stock is now priced at a high-single-digit percentage premium.

One can appreciate the merit of paying that premium if VZ was on course to deliver exceptional earnings growth that would imply a PEG (Price-to-earnings growth) ratio of around 1x or thereabouts. However, that’s not quite what you’re going to see this year, with expected EPS growth of less than 2% (implying a relatively high PEG of over 5x)!

Thus, to conclude, we are now revising our rating on VZ from a Buy to a Hold.

Performance Review

The Fund (Institutional Class) returned -0.90 percent for the fourth quarter, outperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, by 0.32 percent. This brought the net year-to-date absolute and relative performance to 3.01 percent and 1.96 percent, respectively. Longer-duration products detracted from performance as rates sold off during the quarter, with tax-exempt agency commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) and zero-coupon bonds giving back a portion of their third quarter outperformance. Among the most heavily represented states in the portfolio, New York bonds contributed the most to total return, while those from Virginia and Tennessee detracted from returns for the period.

Strategy and Positioning

The market began 2025 with expectations of a large new issue calendar, while discussions ramped up in Washington D.C. over tax regime changes and their potential impact on municipal issuers’ access to tax-exempt financing.

Valuations remained tight despite widespread uncertainty. We believe traditional high quality sectors, such as general obligation property tax bonds and utilities, do not offer attractive risk/reward at current muni/Treasury yield ratios.

Instead, we are looking for solid credits within higher beta sectors, including affordable housing, charter schools, and continuing care retirement communities (CCRCs).

QTD Contributors / Detractors

Longer-duration products detracted as rates sold off during the quarter. Tax exempt Agency CMBS detracted. Zero-coupon bonds detracted. New York bonds contributed to performance, while Virginia and Tennessee detracted.

Economic and Market Review

The U.S. economy has good momentum heading into 2025, but the policy outlook from Washington elevates uncertainty. Recent economic data have been solid, with fourth quarter real gross domestic product (GDP) on track for about 2 percent annualized growth. The outlook for consumer spending remains positive, supported by healthy growth in inflation-adjusted labor income and a wealth effect driven by rising asset prices. Financial conditions have also turned more supportive as credit growth is reaccelerating and optimism about artificial intelligence induces a positive outlook for capex. Disinflationary progress has stalled a bit in recent months, but fundamentals point to a further slowdown in inflation as wage pressures and housing inflation ease further. With the new administration taking office, investors want to know how the evolving policy landscape will alter the outlook. In the immediate future, we expect a boost to both consumer and business sentiment, aided by expectations of deregulation and further tax cuts. Post-election surveys have already shown increased optimism about the outlook, which could support consumption, investment, and hiring in coming months. Looking beyond the immediate sentiment boost, the outlook becomes more uncertain and depends on the ultimate policy mix of the new administration. Extension of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) would prevent a fiscal drag, but we see limited scope for new tax cuts as the fiscal backdrop has worsened. Broad tariffs could weigh on growth and push up prices, though we expect more targeted tariffs will ultimately be used.

All together, we see moderate growth in the U.S. economy in 2025 as these policy shifts play out. Economic fundamentals remain solid, with strong household and corporate balance sheets. The Federal Reserve (Fed) will likely ease policy further toward a neutral setting, but tariffs could slow the pace of rate cuts by interrupting the disinflationary trend. The U.S. economy should remain a global outperformer, though we expect continued bifurcation across sectors, particularly as new policies begin to have an impact.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Please see page 2 for additional performance information.

Certain sectors of the municipal bond market have special risks that can affect them more significantly than the market as a whole. Because many municipal instruments are issued to finance similar projects, conditions in these industries can significantly affect the fund and the overall municipal market.

  • Municipalities currently experience budget shortfalls, which could cause them to default on their debt and thus subject the fund to unforeseen losses.
  • Like other funds that hold bonds and other fixed-income investments, the fund’s market value will change in response to interest rate changes and market conditions, among other factors. In general, bond prices rise when interest rates fall and vice versa.

  • The fund may invest in derivative instruments, which may be more volatile and less liquid, increasing the risk of loss when compared to traditional securities. Certain of the derivative instruments are also subject to the risks of counterparty default and adverse tax treatment.
  • Instruments and strategies (such as reverse repurchase agreements, unfunded commitments, tender option bonds and borrowings) may expose the fund to many of the same risks as investments in derivatives and may provide leveraged exposure to a particular investment, which will magnify any gains or losses on those investments.
  • The fund’s investments in other investment vehicles subject the fund to those risks and expenses affecting the investment vehicle.
  • The fund’s investments in foreign securities carry additional risks when compared to U.S. securities, due to the impact of diplomatic, political or economic developments in the country in question (investments in emerging markets securities are generally subject to an even greater level of risks).
